coredata.dc:title: Three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot/Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/nickel foam nanocomposites as ultra-high-efficiency cathode electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction applications

coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shu-Hua
Wu, Chia-Ching
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed a three-dimensional (3D) nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/nickel foam (NF) structure as a high-performance electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) applications. This novel structure exhibited a high surface area, abundant electrochemically active sites, and efficient electronic transmission networks. In alkaline solution, the proposed structure demonstrated an overpotential of 112 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, along with a low Tafel slope of 28 mV dec−1, indicating excellent HER activity. Furthermore, the structure exhibited stability over 100 h during durability testing. Overall, this innovative structure experimentally confirms that Cu-based materials can serve as efficient electrocatalysts for HER applications.

head.ce:title: Three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot/Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/nickel foam nanocomposites as ultra-high-efficiency cathode electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Development of Applied Science, National Taitung University, Taiwan, ROC
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Development of Applied Science, National Taitung University, Taiwan, R. O. C.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed a three-dimensional (3D) nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/nickel foam (NF) structure as a high-performance electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) applications. This novel structure exhibited a high surface area, abundant electrochemically active sites, and efficient electronic transmission networks. In alkaline solution, the proposed structure demonstrated an overpotential of 112 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, along with a low Tafel slope of 28 mV dec−1, indicating excellent HER activity. Furthermore, the structure exhibited stability over 100 h during durability testing. Overall, this innovative structure experimentally confirms that Cu-based materials can serve as efficient electrocatalysts for HER applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical water splitting is an attractive, renewable, and eco-friendly energy storage technology for generating hydrogen fuel, which serves as an ideal energy source due to its high energy density and pollution-free utilization [1]. In recent decades, photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) and electrocatalytic (EC) water splitting have been regarded as reliable processes for the mass production of oxygen and hydrogen [2,3]. Among renewable energy sources, solar energy is particularly attractive due to its abundant supply, which provides the power necessary for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). PEC uses light energy to drive catalysts for the decomposition of water molecules. This process combines the light absorption properties of semiconductor materials with the catalytic reactivity, enabling the direct splitting of water into H₂ and O₂ under illumination.

Ideal photoanodes should possess key characteristics such as broad spectral absorption and low bulk/surface recombination to effectively facilitate the water oxidation reaction. Narrow bandgap semiconductor materials (e.g., BiVO₄, Ta₃N₅, Fe₂O₃, CdIn₂S₄) are effective at extending spectral absorption into the visible light region [4–6]. Additionally, the light-harvesting capabilities of some wide bandgap semiconductors (e.g., TiO₂, ZnO, WO₃) have been enhanced through various optimization methods [7–8]. However, the current efficiency of photocatalytic technology remains relatively low and has yet to meet the demands of large-scale commercialization.

In terms of EC, hydrogen production primarily occurs through water electrolysis, which involves two half-reactions: the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode and the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode. Electrocatalysis offers high energy conversion efficiency and controlled reaction conditions, making it well-suited for efficient chemical conversions when a stable power supply is available. Electrocatalytic water electrolysis technology is relatively mature and has achieved a higher degree of commercialization [9,10]. Commercial HER and OER electrocatalysts are typically precious metal-containing nanomaterials, such as Pt/C, RuO₂, and IrO₂ [11,12]. However, the high cost and limited stability under certain conditions are significant drawbacks associated with these electrocatalysts. Therefore, the development of efficient electrocatalysts with high activity, long-term stability, and relatively low cost is crucial for practical applications in renewable energy devices.

Over the past decades, extensive research has focused on developing highly efficient, non-noble HER and OER electrocatalysts, particularly those based on 3d transition metals such as Co, Ni, Fe, and Mn, which are known for their high performance [13,14]. Cu-based nanomaterials, widely utilized in various fields, including optics, electronics, and biochemistry applications [15–17], have also shown promising performance in HER and OER in several studies [18–20]. The diverse redox properties and low cost of Cu-based nanomaterials have significantly advanced the field of electrocatalysis [21–23]. Cu-based oxides exhibit versatile reactions owing to the various oxidation states of Cu (Cu0, Cu+, Cu2+, and Cu3+), making them useful in many potential applications. Cu₂O, a 3d transition-metal oxide with intrinsic properties, is abundant, non-toxic, and an excellent environmentally friendly alternative for various catalytic applications. Its high absorption coefficient in the visible region makes it suitable for both electrocatalytic [24,25] and photocatalytic [26,27] redox reactions. Specifically, when Cu₂O is irradiated with photons of an appropriate wavelength, electrons in the valence band are excited to the conduction band, generating photogenerated electrons and holes that drive photocatalytic redox reactions.

While many studies have explored the preparation and application of Cu₂O as an electrocatalysts for OERs, its electrochemical properties as a electrocatalysts for HERs have not been adequately investigated. In HERs, electrocatalytic water splitting in alkaline solutions is widely regarded as a standard process for converting electrical energy into clean and recyclable hydrogen energy [28]. Currently, the application of Cu₂O-based electrocatalysts in HERs is limited, and their performance in practical applications remains unsatisfactory.

Recent studies have shown that the electrocatalytic performance of Cu₂O-based materials can be enhanced by modifying their microstructure, morphology, and chemical composition. For example, three-dimensional (3D) Cu/Cu₂O-CuO/rGO composites [29], Cu-Cu₂O nanoparticles in a carbon matrix (Cu-Cu₂O NPs/C) [30], CuO@Cu₂O nanowires [31], RGOL/PPS/CNT + RGO-Cu₂O films [32], and Cu₂O octahedrons inlaid in Ni foam [33] have been developed to achieve lower overpotentials and higher electrocatalytic activity. Cu₂O is a wide-bandgap (2.2 eV) p-type semiconductor, and depositing precious metals such as La, Pt, Au, Ru, and Pd on its surface has been shown to enhance the separation of photogenerated electron (e−) and hole (h+) pairs. Although the illumination of Cu₂O nanostructures with solar light did not enhance electrocatalytic OER performance, the deposition of Au nanoparticles on Cu₂O's surface improved OER activity, achieving an overpotential of 200 mV (10 mA cm−2) and a Tafel slope of 97 mV dec−1, despite the high cost of Au [34]. Shen et al. demonstrated that Ru-doped Cu₂O exhibits superior HER performance due to its specific morphology, superhydrophilic surface, porous support, and abundant active sites. The Ru@Cu₂O electrocatalyst showed a low Tafel slope of 50 mV dec−1 and a small overpotential of 31 mV at 10 mA cm−2 [35]. Additionally, other studies have discussed the impact of metal morphology on HER characteristics. For instance, Ag-Ni core–shell nanowires displayed superior electrocatalytic activity compared to pure Ni nanoparticles, Ag-Ni core–shell nanoparticles, and Ag nanowires, highlighting a strong synergistic effect toward the alkaline HER [36]. Ag-based nanoparticles are effective in both OERs and HERs due to their high conductivity, large surface-to-volume ratio, and ability to form highly faceted Ag crystal structures [37]. Moreover, Ag is more readily available and less expensive than other precious metals.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess a conjugated structure and exhibit unique properties, including low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and superior electron transfer and reservoir capabilities [38]. Introducing heteroatoms into the framework of carbon materials is a versatile and effective strategy for modifying their structural and electronic properties, thereby enhancing their overall electrocatalytic performance in HERs and OERs. Specifically, nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) are quantum dots with favorable conductive properties that induce charge delocalization and reduce the work function of graphene [39]. Lin et al. reported that NGQD deposition on the surface of bimetallic CoFe phosphide (CoFeP) and cobalt iron Prussian blue analogue (CoFePBA) resulted in an efficient HER, as the surface architecture and electronic state of the transition metal were altered through an adjusted electronic configuration when NGQDs were introduced [40]. Additionally, in NiCu-POx/NGQD composites, the enhanced electrical conductivity achieved by uniformly incorporating NGQDs into the porous NiCo-MOF matrix led to the development of highly efficient electrocatalysts for HER applications [41].

To promote HERs, efficient electrocatalysts with low overpotential, high activity, and excellent stability are essential. In this study, Cu₂O-Cu trunk electrocatalysts were grown in situ on 3D nickel foam (NF) with a high specific surface area to form a 3D electrocatalyst electrode with high electrical conductivity, a larger active surface area, and enhanced stability. Most studies have focused on fabricating powder electrocatalysts that are subsequently pasted onto a conductive substrate using Nafion binders to form a working electrode. However, the use of binders may reduce electrocatalytic performance [42]. Moreover, photogenerated e− and h+ pairs can hinder the process of recombination by modifying Cu₂O. Therefore, in this study, Cu₂O was modified by depositing a Ag leaf nanostructure on its surface to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated e− and h+ pairs. Additionally, NGQDs were deposited on the surface of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure to enhance surface active sites and electron transfer capability. Finally, the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure can overcome the drawback of low efficiency in Cu-based electrocatalyst materials for HER applications.

Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2, EG), Silver nitrate (AgNO3), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), Citric acid (C6H8O7), Urea ((NH2)2CO)) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Nickel foam was purchased from MTI Korea. Alcohol was purchased from Uni-Onward Corp. All of the other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of a Cu2O-Cu trunk structure
ce:section.ce:para: Before the electrodeposition of Cu2O-Cu trunks, NF was soaked for 20 min in a 1 M solution of HCl, acetone, ethanol, and deionized water to remove the oxides on its surface. After vacuum drying at 60 °C, NF was immersed in a mixture of 0.125 M CuSO4·5H2O and 0.5 M H2SO4. Subsequently, electrodeposition was performed using a galvanostatic system, in which NF was directly used as the working electrode, and a platinum line was used as the counter electrode. Cu2O-Cu trunks were fabricated at different electrochemical deposition current densities on NF at 25 °C for 180 s.

Briefly, (CH2OH)2 was heated at 160 °C for 1 h, and 0.25 M AgNO3 and 0.375 M PVP were added at the same temperature for 45 min. After centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 20 min, Ag nanoparticles were obtained.

The 1 mmol C6H8O7 (as a carbon source) and 4 mmol (NH2)2CO) (as a nitrogen source) were placed in a beaker and dissolved in 5 mL of water. After the mixture was transferred to a heating plate and heated at 140 °C for 3 h, it was cooled down to room temperature. Alcohol was added to the beaker to rinse the solid product, and the mixture was dried at 60 °C to obtain a powder. Finally, the powder was dissolved in 5 mL of dimethylformamide to obtain a solution of NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of an NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure
ce:section.ce:para: The 0.1 wt% PVP solution was heated at 90 °C, and Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF was added to the solution, with stirring for 10 min. Subsequently, Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF was immersed in a Ag nanoparticle solution, and a Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure was obtained through conventional redox displacement. The Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF nanocomposite structure was annealed in a tube furnace in a vacuum environment at 400 °C for 2 h. Finally, the Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure was placed in an NGQD solution for 30 min and annealed at 140 °C for 1 h in a vacuum environment to obtain the final NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure.

High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8000, Japan) and ultrahigh-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2100F, Japan) were used to examine the morphology of the proposed NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 DISCOVER/Bruker, Germany) with CuKα radiation and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC-PHI/PHI 5000, Japan) were used to determine the crystal structure and binding energy of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure. HER curve measurements were conducted using a three-electrode system with a potentiostat/galvanostat electrochemical analyzer (AMETEK VersaSTAT 3, USA) at room temperature. In this setup, Hg/HgO served as the reference electrode, a graphite rod was used as the counter electrode, and the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF-related structures were employed as the working electrode. The reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the conversion equation in 1 M KOH solution, E (RHE) = E (Hg/HgO) + 0.924 V for Hg/HgO as the reference electrode.

Figs. 1 and S1 present the SEM images of a Cu2O-Cu trunk fabricated on NF under different electrochemical deposition current densities. NF was selected as the substrate due to its 3D structure, high surface area, excellent conductivity, and self-supporting characteristics. As illustrated in the low-magnification SEM images in Fig. S1, NF exhibited a micro-sized porous structure. Fig. S1(a) to (d) demonstrates that as the electrochemical deposition current density increased from 0.5 to 2.0 A/cm2, the extent of the Cu2O-Cu trunk growth on NF increased significantly. Fig. 1 shows high-magnification SEM images of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure at varying electrochemical current densities. As the electrochemical current density increased from 0.5 to 2.0 A/cm2, the Cu2O-Cu trunk developed a more complete and well-defined structure.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the Cu2O-Cu trunk was synthesized using an electrochemical deposition method employing a dynamic hydrogen bubble template (DHBT). During the deposition process, a significant number of hydrogen bubbles formed on the cathode surface, acting as a dynamic template for the Cu2O-Cu trunk growth on NF (Fig. 2a). The hydrogen bubbles evolved at the cathode surface due to the depletion of metal ions, preventing Cu deposition at the bubble sites. Only the regions between the hydrogen bubbles allowed for Cu ion deposition. As the electrochemical current density increases, the concentration of Cu ions between the bubbles rises. The Cu ions are reduced and form metallic Cu, resulting in a thicker Cu layer on the NF. When the electrochemical deposition current density was further increased, the number of hydrogen bubbles increased, and they coalesced and became larger, resulting in a complete Cu trunk shape, as shown in Fig. 2(b) to (e). During the electrochemical deposition process, the outer layers of the Cu trunk were spontaneously converted into Cu2O in the presence of an aqueous electrolyte, resulting in the formation of a Cu2O-Cu core-shell structure, as shown in Fig. 2(d) and (e). The detailed chemical reaction processes and equations for the Cu₂O-Cu trunk structures are provided in the Supporting Information.

Fig. 3 presents TEM images of a Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at an electrochemical deposition current density of 2 A/cm2. Fig. 3(a) shows the tip of the Cu2O-Cu trunk structure, while Fig. 3(b) displays a high-magnification TEM image revealing a uniform core-shell structure, with the Cu2O shell approximately 16–18 nm thick. The lattice distance of the core portion of the Cu2O-Cu trunk was measured at 2.04 Å, corresponding to the (111) plane of Cu. For the shell portion, the lattice distances were 2.49 Å and 2.13 Å, corresponding to the (111) and (200) planes of Cu2O, respectively. These lattice distances are consistent with the theoretical values calculated from Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) files no. 04-0836 and no. 65-3288. Fig. 3(c) and (d) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of the Cu2O-Cu trunk structure. The diffraction spots for Cu and Cu2O observed in Fig. 3(a) and (b) indicate that both materials have a monocrystalline structure.

Fig. 4(a) presents the XRD pattern of a Cu2O-Cu trunk fabricated on NF at various electrochemical deposition current densities. The diffraction peaks of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure appear at 2θ = 29.7°, 36.3°, 42.7°, 61.3°, and 73.9°, corresponding to the (110), (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of Cu2O, respectively. Additionally, peaks corresponding to the (111) and (200) planes of Cu are also observed in the XRD pattern. These results are in agreement with the standard JCPDS data for Cu2O (JCPDS no. 05-0667) and Cu (JCPDS no. 65-9743). Notably, no diffraction peaks were detected for CuO. The peaks at 2θ = 44.6°, 51.6°, and 76.4° correspond to NF, representing the (111), (200), and (220) planes of Ni (JCPDS no. 87-0712).

As shown in Fig. 4(a), the intensity of the Cu2O diffraction peaks increased, while the intensity of the NF diffraction peaks decreased with increasing electrochemical deposition current density. These observations align with the SEM results and support the proposed growth mechanism of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structures. The results also confirm that the primary crystal phase of Cu2O is (111). According to Huang et al. [43], Cu2O crystals with the (111) phase exhibit high conductivity and enhanced activity for HERs.

In this study, a Cu2O-Cu trunk was fabricated on NF at various electrochemical deposition current densities, resulting in a Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF electrocatalyst. The potential of this structure as an electrocatalyst for HERs was subsequently evaluated (Fig. 4(b)–(c)). A three-electrode system was employed to assess the HER performance of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure in 1 M KOH, using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scan rate of 1 mV/s. Our results confirmed that the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure is effective for HER measurements without the need for additional Nafion binders or extra substrates. To facilitate comparison, similar measurements were performed for both the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF and NF structures. Fig. S2 displays the LSV curves and Tafel slope of the NF structures. Pristine NF exhibited limited HER activity within the potential range of 0.75–1.25 V (vs. RHE), with an overpotential and Tafel slope of 352 mV and 191 mV dec−1, respectively, at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 (Fig. S2(a) and (b)). Fig. 4(b) shows the LSV curves of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structures with different electrochemical deposition current density after iR compensation. The Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at 2 A/cm2 electrochemical deposition current density with and without iR compensation as shown in Fig. S3. In Fig. 4(b), the current density of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at an electrochemical deposition current density of 2 A/cm2 was higher than that of the structures fabricated at 0.5–1.5 A/cm2 for the same overpotential. Additionally, the overpotential of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at 2 A/cm2 (239 mV dec−1) was significantly lower compared to those fabricated at 1.5 A/cm2 (272 mV dec−1), 1 A/cm2 (319 mV dec−1), and 0.5 A/cm2 (347 mV dec−1) at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. This demonstrates the superior HER electrocatalytic activity of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at 2 A/cm2.

To gain further insights into the kinetics of HERs, the Tafel slopes of various electrocatalyst were extracted from the LSV results. As shown in Fig. S2(b), NF exhibited a Tafel slope of 191 mV dec−1. Fig. 4(c) presents the Tafel slopes of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structures fabricated at different electrochemical deposition current densities. The Tafel slope for the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at 2 A/cm2 (132 mV dec−1) was significantly lower than those of the structures fabricated at 0.5–1.5 A/cm2. A smaller Tafel slope generally indicates a lower kinetic barrier, leading to a faster HER rate. Our results demonstrated that the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at 2 A/cm2 exhibited the highest HER activity, the lowest overpotential, and the smallest Tafel slope among all the tested structures. This is because the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF trunk structure became more completed, and Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF served as the primary electrocatalytic active site for HER, as metallic Cu alone exhibits poor electrocatalyst performance for HER.

To further enhance the HER electrocatalytic activity and increase the specific surface area of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure fabricated at an electrochemical deposition current density of 2 A/cm2, an Ag leaf nanostructure was deposited on the surface of the Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF, followed by annealing at 400 °C in a vacuum environment. Subsequently, NGQDs were deposited on the surface of the Ag leaf/Cu2O-Cu trunk/NF structure.

Fig. S4(a) shows an SEM image of the Ag leaf on the surface of the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. The Ag dendritic branches have an average length and width of approximately 1.5 μm and 0.6 μm, respectively. In the formation mechanism of the Ag leaf nanostructures, ethylene glycol (EG) acts as a strong reducing agent, capable of reducing most metal salts to their elemental metal forms, which is favorable for the formation of dendritic nanostructures. At high temperatures, EG (CH₂OH–CH₂OH) can decompose into a reducing agent, glycolic aldehyde (HOCH₂CHO), which contains two aldehyde groups (–CHO). As a result, the reducing agent solution was added dropwise to the Ag+ solution, facilitating the reduction of Ag+ ions to silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) through the following chemical reactions:

These Ag NPs come into contact with one another, forming larger structures such as dendrites. Additionally, at higher Ag NP concentrations, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was added to prevent significant aggregation of the Ag NPs. PVP is a water-soluble material containing a large number of amino groups, which can bond with metal ions, forming a unique PVP–Ag complex precursor system in solution. In general, using amine complexes of silver as precursors is an effective strategy for controlling the reduction of silver ions. These complexes facilitate the formation of Ag dendritic nanostructures. Fig. S4(b) shows a high-resolution TEM image of NGQDs. The NGQDs, derived from a mixture of citric acid and urea, have an average size of approximately 6 nm. The high-resolution TEM images indicate a lattice spacing of about 0.22 nm, consistent with the (100) plane of graphite.

Fig. 5 shows focused ion beam–SEM (FIB-SEM) imaging and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results for elemental mapping of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. Fig. 5(a) shows an FIB-SEM image, where the Cu₂O-Cu trunk appears in the charcoal gray area, and the Ag leaf appears in the light gray area. It was observed that the Ag leaf and Cu₂O-Cu trunk are connected, confirming that the Ag leaf is deposited on the Cu₂O-Cu trunk. Fig. 5(b)–(d) displays the EDS elemental mapping analysis of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. The elements Cu and O are uniformly distributed in the charcoal gray area, corresponding to the Cu₂O-Cu trunk, while the element Ag is uniformly distributed in the light gray area, corresponding to the Ag leaf.

Fig. 6(a) shows the XRD pattern of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. The diffraction peaks of the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure, fabricated at an electrochemical deposition current density of 2 A/cm2, are also presented in this figure. This pattern is the same as the one shown in Fig. 4(a). As seen in Fig. 6(a), the diffraction peaks of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure are similar to those of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. The diffraction peaks corresponding to Cu₂O were observed at 2θ = 29.7°, 36.3°, 42.7°, 61.3°, and 73.9°, while those corresponding to Cu were observed at 2θ = 43.2° and 50.4°. Peaks at 2θ = 38.1°, 64.4°, and 77.3° correspond to the (111), (200), and (311) planes of Ag, respectively, according to JCPDS no. 04-0783. However, no clear diffraction peaks for the NGQDs were observed, as the NGQDs exhibited a broad band at 2θ = 25.3°, with an intensity significantly lower than that of Ag, Cu₂O, Cu, and NF.

Fig. 6(b) presents the LSV curves of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, and Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures in 1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 1 mV/s with iR compensation. In Fig. 6(b), the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure, fabricated at a electrochemical deposition current density of 10 mA/cm2, exhibited an overpotential of 129 mV, which is significantly lower than that of the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure (239 mV) at the same current density. This improvement is attributed to the high electrical conductivity and large surface area of the Ag leaf, which facilitates rapid electron transfer from the Ag leaf to the Cu₂O-Cu trunk.

As shown in Fig. S5, the overpotential of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure decreased further in the LSV curves as the annealing temperature increased from 100 °C to 400 °C, due to improved crystallization and enhanced electrical conductivity of the Ag leaf. According to a previous study [44], Au nanoparticles can enhance the electrocatalytic properties of Cu₂O crystals with a (111) phase. In this study, the enhanced electrocatalytic activity of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure is attributed to the synergetic effects between the Ag leaf and Cu₂O, where the predominant crystal phase of Cu₂O is (111). Furthermore, the uniform distribution of NGQDs on the surface of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure further reduced the overpotential, resulting in an overpotential of 112 mV for the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. Among the three electrocatalysts tested, the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure exhibited the lowest onset potential for HERs and the highest current density at the same overpotential, indicating the highest HER activity.

Fig. 6(c) shows the Tafel slopes of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, and Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures. The NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure exhibited a Tafel slope of approximately 28 mV dec−1, which is lower than those of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF and Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures, at 51 mV dec−1 and 132 mV dec−1, respectively. The slightly smaller Tafel slope of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF compared to the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure suggests that the number of active sites in the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure is marginally higher than in the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. In general, catalytic activity is closely related to the number of active sites, which in turn depends on the specific surface area. In this study, the specific surface area of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure was slightly larger than that of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure, contributing to the increased number of active sites.

Fig. S6 provides an extended comparison of HER performance at three different current densities (10, 20, and 50 mA/cm2). The NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure exhibited overpotentials of 112 mV, 120 mV, and 143 mV, and Tafel slopes of 28, 35, and 53 mV dec−1 for current densities of 10, 20, and 50 mA/cm2, respectively. The overpotentials and Tafel slopes of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure increased slightly as the current density rose from 10 to 50 mA/cm2. In contrast, the overpotentials (239 mV, 295 mV, 378 mV) and Tafel slopes (132, 148, 198 mV dec−1) of the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure showed a rapid increase over the same current density range. A similar trend was observed for the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. These results indicate that the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure demonstrates more stable HER performance compared to the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF and Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures. The 3D NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure enhances gas release and accelerates the electron transfer rate, contributing to its superior stability.

The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of an electrocatalyst is a crucial parameter, as it determines the extent to which the active sites of positive or negative ions are exposed to the electrode surface when anodic or cathodic potentials are applied in a non-faradaic region. The area of exposed active sites is directly related to the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the modified working electrode. In this study, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed in a non-faradaic region at various scan rates to determine the double-layer capacitance.

As shown by the CV in Fig. 6(d), the current density increased with increasing scan rates. A single potential value was calculated from the detected value within the non-faradaic capacitive potential window. In the range of 2 to 100 mV/s, plotting anodic current against the scan rate produced a straight line, with the slope corresponding to Cdl. The calculated Cdl value for the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O trunk/NF structure was 113.34 mF/cm2, exceeding those of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O trunk/NF, Cu₂O trunk/NF, and NF structures, confirming the large specific surface area of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O trunk/NF structure, as illustrated in Fig. 6(e).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to evaluate the improvement in electrochemical activity following the deposition of NGQDs and the Ag leaf onto the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. As shown in Fig. 6(f), the EIS spectra exhibit a semicircle in the high-frequency region, which corresponds to the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) arising from the redox reactions occurring on the surface of the electrocatalyst. Compared with the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure (Rct = 4.45 Ω), the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF (Rct = 3.38 Ω) and NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF (Rct = 2.76 Ω) structures showed lower Rct values. This reduction in Rct indicates that the presence of NGQDs and the Ag leaf significantly enhanced electron transfer from the substrate to the electrolyte.

According to the results mentioned above, the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure exhibited high HER activity, which can be attributed to the following mechanisms. Typically, the kinetics of HER follow three primary mechanisms: the Volmer mechanism, the Heyrovsky mechanism, and the Tafel mechanism [45,46].

Volmer mechanism:
(1)
M +
H 2
O +
e −
→ M − H +
OH −

Heyrovsky mechanism:
(2)
M − H +
H 2
O +
e −
→ M +
OH −
+
H 2

Tafel mechanism:
(3)
M − H + M − H → 2
M +
H 2

Here, M represents the electrocatalyst, and H denotes the hydrogen atom adsorbed on the electrocatalyst's surface. When an Ag leaf or NGQDs/Ag leaf is deposited onto a Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure, the electronic interaction between the Ag leaf or NGQDs/Ag leaf and Cu₂O induces a positively charged Ag leaf or NGQD/Ag leaf. This positively charged surface in the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure can adsorb and activate H₂O molecules by capturing the oxygen atoms from H₂O through the Volmer mechanism. The synergistic effects of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure optimize both the adsorption and desorption of hydrogen atoms, facilitating efficient HER through the Heyrovsky mechanism. The adsorption of H₂O molecules on the surface of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, forming the electrocatalyst-H₂O complex, is significantly more favorable than that on the Cu₂O-Cu trunk alone, further enhancing the HER process. Fig. 7 illustrates a schematic of the hydrogen production steps.

To further understand the HER mechanism, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to estimate the adsorption energies and Gibbs free energy changes (ΔGH*) for hydrogen atom adsorption under alkaline conditions. Fig. S7 shows the calculated ΔGH* values for H* adsorption on Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, and NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures. The results indicate that both NGQD and Ag exhibit positive ΔGH* values, suggesting weak hydrogen adsorption and consequently lower HER activity. In contrast, the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF, and NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures display lower ΔGH* values, with the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure showing particularly strong H* adsorption. These DFT calculations demonstrate that the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure has the potential for high HER efficiency.

XPS analysis was used to determine the surface state of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure exhibited two peaks at 931.9 and 951.7 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p. Peak deconvolution of the Cu 2p spectrum through Gaussian fitting revealed characteristic Cu+, Cu2+, and satellite peaks at 931.9/951.7, 933.2/953.1, and 944.6/962.7 eV, respectively. Since Cu+ is present in both Cu₂O and Cu, the intensity of the Cu+ peak was higher than those of Cu2+ and the satellite peaks. However, the deconvolution process could not resolve Cu₂O from Cu because their binding energies were very close, differing by only 0.1 eV [47]. In Fig. 8(b), the peaks at 371 and 377 eV correspond to Ag 3d₅/₂ and Ag 3d₃/₂, as observed in the Ag 3d spectra. Gaussian fitting of the Ag 3d spectrum revealed the characteristic Ag0 (metallic Ag). Furthermore, no peaks were detected for Ag-O 3d₅/₂ and Ag-O 3d₃/₂ at 373 and 379 eV, respectively, indicating that no oxidation occurred on the surface of the Ag leaf [48]. The O 1s spectra, analyzed through Gaussian fitting, showed characteristic OCu (Cu2+), OCu (Cu+), and OC peaks in the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure. The intensity of the OCu (Cu2+) and OCu (Cu+) peaks corresponded with the results of the Cu 2p spectra, as shown in Fig. 8(c) [49]. The OC peak originated from the NGQDs. Additionally, the C 1s spectra, also analyzed via Gaussian fitting, confirmed the successful deposition of NGQDs on the surface of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure, as shown in Fig. 8(d) [50].

In addition to catalytic activity, the stability of the electrocatalyst plays a crucial role in commercial water-splitting systems. To evaluate the stability and durability of the proposed NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure, a chronopotentiometric analysis was performed over 100 h at a constant current density of 20 mA/cm2 for HER (Fig. 9 ). The results showed that the Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure maintained a stable voltage of 0.431 V for approximately 30 h, with its potential decreasing slightly by 5.52 % from 30 to 100 h. The potential values of the Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF and NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structures exhibited minimal changes, indicating that these structures remained stable throughout the catalytic process, as shown in Fig. 9.

After 100 h of durability testing, XPS analysis was conducted to examine the Cu 2p spectra of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure (Fig. S8). As seen in Fig. S8(b), the Cu 2p spectra after 100 h of testing showed characteristic Cu+, Cu2+, and satellite peaks at 931.9/951.7, 933.2/953.1, and 944.6/962.7 eV, respectively. The peak intensity of Cu+ remained higher than that of Cu2+ and the satellite peaks, consistent with the results in Fig. S8(a) before the durability test. Additionally, after the durability test, the XRD diffraction peaks of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure showed no significant changes, as illustrated in Fig. S9. These results indicate that the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure retained its structural integrity and catalytic properties after 100 h of durability testing. It is mean that the 3D NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure enhances gas release and accelerates the electron transfer rate, contributing to its superior stability.

Fig. 10 illustrates the relationship between overpotential and the Tafel slope (derived from the LSV curves at a current density of 10 mA/cm2) for Cu-based electrocatalysts [51–56]. As a hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) electrocatalyst, the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure demonstrated superior performance, exhibiting both a lower overpotential and Tafel slope compared to most reported electrocatalysts, particularly those based on Cu₂O. In comparison to other metal-based electrocatalysts, the Tafel slope of the NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure is lower than that of WO₂-, MoO₂-, TiO₂-, RuO₂-, MoS₂-, and Co₃O₄-based electrocatalysts, as shown in Fig. S10. This indicates that the 3D NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure enhances electron transfer rates and increases the number of active sites, contributing to its high HER efficiency and exceptional stability. Overall, the 3D NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure exhibits excellent electrocatalytic properties, making it a promising candidate for commercialization.

In this study, we fabricated a 3D NGQD/Ag leaf/Cu₂O-Cu trunk/NF structure as a highly efficient electrocatalyst, characterized by a large surface area, abundant active sites, long-term stability, and excellent performance. At a current density of 10 mA/cm2, the proposed structure exhibited an overpotential of 112 mV and a Tafel slope of 28 mV dec−1, maintaining high durability for 100 h in an alkaline solution. This outstanding electrocatalytic performance can be attributed to the unique 3D design of the nanostructure, which facilitates rapid electron transfer, enhances electrolyte penetration, and shortens diffusion paths for electrolytes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the most effective Cu₂O-based electrocatalyst for HER reported to date.
